approved.title: Antitrust Undone: How Competition Enforcers Are Undermining Competition
approved.summary: Argues that structuralist antitrust approaches (both the EU's Digital Markets Act and US 'antitrust populism') risk overenforcement that harms innovation and consumer welfare. Claims the EU model's 'big is bad' bias has caused Europe's tech economy to underperform relative to the US since the 1970s. Contends that platform/start-up merger challenges have caused collateral damage, citing the iRobot bankruptcy and Autotalks being acquired at a fraction of its original value. Concludes that returning to structural presumptions would risk 'a policy error of significant proportions.'
approved.whyBad: Analysis: [1. The paper's Key Takeaways present the EU model as having 'a poor track record in supporting the dynamic forces of entrepreneurship and innovation' and asserts a causal relationship between EU competition policy and Europe's tech deficit. 2. This commits a post hoc ergo propter hoc fallacy — observing that Europe has fewer tech giants than the US and concluding that EU competition policy (not differences in capital markets, venture capital depth, university-industry linkages, labor mobility, language fragmentation, or single-market incompleteness) is the cause. Notably, the body text at section 'The Overlooked Error Costs' hedges ('To be clear, there are multiple factors...') while the Key Takeaways present the causation as settled — a framing discrepancy between abstract and concrete arguments. 3. Additional fallacies: ecological fallacy (inferring individual firm dynamics from aggregate transatlantic comparisons) and selection bias (citing iRobot and Autotalks as cautionary tales while omitting cases where merger enforcement preserved competition, e.g., Microsoft/Activision remedies). The paper also fails to engage with the Draghi Report's identification of fragmented capital markets, lower R&D spending, and regulatory barriers — not primarily competition policy — as drivers of Europe's innovation gap. 4. The piece advances and normalizes harm by providing intellectual cover for antitrust underenforcement, framing structural enforcement as a 'policy error' while minimizing the consumer welfare harms of monopoly power documented in agency complaints.]

approved.title: Biden's Antitrust Overreach Threatens Capitalism
approved.summary: Argues that Biden-era antitrust enforcement against Amazon and Google exceeds the proper scope of the consumer welfare standard and represents a return to a discredited 'big is bad' approach. Claims the FTC's Amazon lawsuit and DOJ's Google suit fail because consumers are pleased with these services and can freely choose alternatives. Concludes that expanded antitrust enforcement 'will lead to politicized enforcement' and 'curtail entrepreneurship, freedom of speech, job creation, investment, and economic prosperity.'
approved.whyBad: Analysis: [1. The article claims 'consumers are pleased with Amazon's services, so this attack against the consumer welfare standard does not appear to be about what is best for customers' and that 'consumers have the agency to take their dollars elsewhere, as Amazon is hardly the only online seller.' 2. This commits the fallacy of conflating consumer satisfaction with competitive conditions — a dominant firm can harm consumer welfare through higher prices, reduced quality, or suppressed innovation even if consumers are 'pleased' relative to no counterfactual. The argument that consumers 'can take their dollars elsewhere' ignores that Amazon controls over 40% of US e-commerce and that network effects and data advantages make meaningful entry extremely difficult. 3. Rhetorical techniques: straw man (framing antitrust enforcement as a binary choice between the consumer welfare standard and 'politicized enforcement,' ignoring the New Brandeisian critique that the consumer welfare standard itself has been underenforced through narrow interpretation); false dichotomy (presenting either no enforcement or 'big-government' politicized enforcement as the only options). 4. The article entirely omits the specifics of the FTC v. Amazon complaint (filed Sept 2023), which alleges specific anticompetitive conduct — including anti-discounting measures that inflate prices across the web — that would harm consumers even under a narrow consumer welfare standard. This selective omission constitutes suppression of contrary evidence. 5. The piece advances harm by normalizing monopoly power as consumer choice, framing market dominance as consumer satisfaction.]

approved.title: Ten Thousand Commandments 2025
approved.summary: Calculates federal regulatory costs at $2.155 trillion annually, claiming this amounts to $16,016 per household in a 'hidden regulatory tax.' Argues that off-budget regulation functions similarly to government spending but escapes congressional oversight. Claims the number of regulations and Federal Register pages hit record highs under Biden, and that the 'Unconstitutionality Index' shows agencies issued 19 rules for every law passed in 2024. Recommends congressional approval of costly agency rules, regulatory sunsetting, and a regulatory reduction commission.
approved.whyBad: Analysis: [1. The study claims US households pay 'on average $16,016 annually in a hidden regulatory tax, which consumes 16 percent of income and 21 percent of household expenses.' 2. This figure aggregates the CEI's own estimate of total regulatory compliance costs ($2.155 trillion) divided by the number of households, but this methodology assumes all regulatory costs are passed to consumers equally and that no benefits (cleaner air, safer food, reduced financial crises) offset those costs. The 'hidden tax' framing rhetorically equates regulation with taxation — an intentional category error that implies regulatory costs are as economically deadweight as taxation, when many regulatory costs produce commensurate or greater benefits. 3. Selection bias/cherry-picking: The report acknowledges the OMB's 2024 draft Report to Congress on Benefits and Costs but only cites the 19 'major' rules that had both benefits and costs quantified ($16.1B in annual benefits), while omitting that the same OMB report found that across all major rules, annual benefits total approximately $916 billion against costs of $94 billion — a roughly 10:1 benefit-to-cost ratio. By selectively reporting only the 19 rules that were fully quantified, the report creates a misleading impression that regulatory benefits are negligible. 4. Additional rhetorical techniques: misleading framing by loaded analogy (calling compliance costs a 'tax' while never applying the same label to the benefits those regulations produce); the report also selectively elevates a more dramatic NAM estimate ($3.079 trillion) while downplaying that the NAM's methodology has been widely criticized by economists for double-counting. 5. The piece advances harm by delegitimizing the regulatory state through one-sided cost accounting, providing a false basis for deregulation that could weaken health, safety, environmental, and financial protections.]

approved.title: In Defense of Billionaires
approved.summary: Argues that billionaires are net positives for society who create enormous value while capturing only a small fraction of it (citing Nordhaus's finding that innovators capture only 2.2% of social returns). Claims three-quarters of the wealthiest 1% own privately held businesses and most billionaires are self-made, not inheritors. Concludes that attacking billionaires sends young people the message that success is bad, potentially lowering aspirations and harming social mobility. Argues the tax system should not be 'weaponized' against any group including billionaires.
approved.whyBad: Analysis: [1. The article states 'Extrapolating from Nordhaus's findings, one could conclude that Bezos has created over $8 trillion — more than one-third of the United States' annual GDP — in value for society.' 2. This extrapolation is mathematically unsound — Nordhaus's 2.2% figure applies to the social returns from technological advances across the entire economy measured at the macro level over decades, not to an individual founder's personal wealth-to-value ratio. The author takes a single macro-level finding about aggregate innovation returns and applies it linearly to an individual's net worth as if the 2.2% rate is a universal constant. This is a false extrapolation (also a form of fallacy of composition). 3. Survivorship bias: The article cites Gates, Bezos, and Zuckerberg while ignoring that the vast majority of start-ups fail, and the structural advantages (access to capital, family wealth, educational privilege) that correlate with extreme success. The 'seven in ten of the wealthiest 400 Americans were self-made' statistic comes from a 2013 paper that defines 'self-made' as not inheriting a controlling business interest — a threshold that ignores intergenerational transfers of wealth in the form of tuition payments, down payments, trust funds, and professional networks. 4. The article conveniently omits that the Saez-Zucman wealth inequality data shows Forbes 400 members collectively saw their wealth increase by $1 trillion in the first year of the pandemic alone — a gain that bears no relation to their individual productivity during a global crisis, undermining the 'productivity = wealth' framing. 5. The 'success is bad' straw man misrepresents critics: the argument is not that success is bad, but that extreme wealth concentration at current levels distorts democracy, entrenches dynastic privilege, and reflects market power as much as productivity. 6. The piece advances harm by providing a pseudo-meritocratic justification for extreme wealth inequality, normalizing billionaire wealth as earned while dismissing structural critiques as jealousy or anti-success messaging.]

approved.title: Tax Cuts (and Tariffs) Need Not Be Paid For
approved.summary: Argues that the Laffer Curve empirically proves tax cuts increase tax revenue over the long term, citing Reagan-era tax receipts and post-TCJA revenue growth. Extends this logic to tariffs, claiming tariff revenues will fully offset the cost of extending TCJA and fund further tax cuts. Concludes the question of 'how tax cuts will be paid for' is a false premise rooted in zero-sum thinking by Democrats and left-leaning economists.
approved.whyBad: Analysis: [1. The article claims 'recent history has shown that tax cuts pay for themselves' and that 'total tax revenues between 1981 and 1990 rose despite tax rate reductions.' It also states federal tax revenue hit $4.9 trillion in 2024, 'up almost $1.5 trillion since 2017,' concluding 'the tax cuts produced higher growth and higher tax revenue collections with lower tax rates.' 2. This commits a false cause fallacy (post hoc ergo propter hoc) — because A (tax cuts) preceded B (rising nominal revenues), the author asserts A caused B, ignoring inflation, population growth, GDP expansion, and other factors independent of rate policy. The article compares nominal revenues without adjusting for inflation or economic growth, and crucially provides no counterfactual (what revenue would have been without the cuts). The CBO has consistently estimated that the TCJA reduced revenues by $1.5-$2 trillion over 10 years relative to the pre-2017 baseline. The article's reference to $4.9 trillion in 2024 collections is meaningless without this comparison. 3. The article then commits a fallacy of composition by extending Laffer Curve logic to tariffs — asserting that since tariffs are 'taxes,' they will similarly 'pay for themselves' via the same growth dynamics — ignoring that tariffs are taxes on imported inputs that raise production costs and reduce economic efficiency, operating in the opposite direction of domestic income tax cuts. 4. Notably, the author self-identifies as a 'MAGA fanatic' whose research was influenced by that status, an admission that ironically undermines the piece's own claim to objective analysis. The article relies on Peter Navarro's $600B/year tariff revenue estimate — a figure that independent analyses (Tax Foundation, Peterson Institute) have found is dramatically overstated and would require tariff rates far exceeding those implemented. 5. External context: The CBO's 2024 long-term budget outlook shows that extending TCJA provisions would increase deficits by $3.9 trillion through 2034 relative to current law, directly contradicting the 'pay for themselves' claim. 6. The piece advances harm by promoting empirically discredited supply-side mythology to justify tax cuts that would increase deficits, and by extending this logic to tariffs — a policy that economists across the political spectrum agree acts as a tax on consumers and reduces economic efficiency.]
